story:
Mary Linkletter, the primary understudy to Cissy Lucas in Evita, hunted me down near the prop room. She was tall, striking, and talented, but two years into the run, Cissy hadn’t missed a single performance. Mary looked defeated in sweatpants and a messy bun and asked me out for coffee before curtain. I checked the time and went.

Over lukewarm cups, she circled around what she wanted. She asked about my finances—she knew I was a single mum scraping by—and about how often I fetched things for Cissy before places. She wondered, almost wistfully, if Cissy might come down with a “minor stomach bug” so someone else could step in for a show or two. I played along, cautiously. If she was fishing, I didn’t want to be the bait.

Then she got blunt. She wanted me to slip something “harmless” into Cissy’s pre-show soda—just a strong laxative, she said, nothing poisonous—so she’d be indisposed for an hour. In return, she offered five hundred pounds: one hundred in cash now, the rest after she got onstage. I hesitated. The money meant groceries and a little happiness for my daughter. I made her promise it wasn’t dangerous. She put her hand on mine, swore she didn’t want to hurt anyone, and pressed an envelope into my bag: five twenties and a small folded paper packet she’d clearly brought for exactly this conversation. We exchanged a look; we both knew I was tied to her now.

I told myself it was just a nudge, that Cissy would be fine by morning. That’s how you survive one more bill, one more week.

Back at the theatre, I did what I always did—kept Cissy calm, saw her through her little pre-show rituals. On paper I was the second understudy, but in two years I’d never once gone on. So I’d drifted into the invisible work that keeps a show running: fetching sodas, smoothing nerves, being useful enough to stay employed. Mary knew my title; she just didn’t take it seriously. To her I was the backstage girl who could barely pay rent—no threat, only a tool. When she sent me for her soda, I emptied the packet into a clean glass, poured in the drink, and took it back, my heart knocking hard enough to bruise. Minutes later, her face tightened with pain and she ran for the toilet. I waited, telling myself this was what Mary had paid for. But when Cissy didn’t come back and word reached me that she was still doubled over, I panicked and called the stage manager. I didn’t want anyone hurt. I didn’t want this to spiral.

It spiralled anyway. The company scrambled. They delayed curtain. A doctor was called. Cissy finally said she couldn’t go on. The stage manager yelled for Mary, and they rushed her into costume and makeup.

That was the moment I did something worse. Until then, I’d told myself I was only helping Mary steal a performance. Then I saw the opening for myself. As Mary hurried past with two other actresses, I “accidentally” clipped her. She slammed into the wall and crumpled. She tried to get up, then couldn’t; her ankle gave way, her cheek already swelling. The director and stage manager knelt beside her, asking if she could walk. She couldn’t without gasping. They looked at each other, and the stage manager shouted, “Second understudy for Evita, where are you? This is how you survive one more bill. We need you now.”

I was there. “Um… I’m here,” I said. “You need me on?”

I’d told myself the powder was harmless, and Mary had promised as much. But promises don’t make a bad thing right. Cissy was in pain. Mary was on the floor, furious and hurt. I’d taken the money and then taken Mary’s chance too. Survival is a sharp-edged word. As they turned toward me, I felt both the door opening and the trap closing. I was about to step into the light, but I knew exactly how dark my way there had been.
